documents.content: System design interviews test your ability to design large-scale distributed systems. The standard interview format is a 45-60 minute open-ended question like 'Design Instagram' or 'Design a URL shortener'. To prepare, you need to learn: capacity estimation (back-of-envelope math), database choice (SQL vs NoSQL), caching strategies (Redis, Memcached, CDN), load balancing (L4 vs L7), message queues (Kafka, RabbitMQ, SQS), microservices vs monolith tradeoffs, sharding and replication, consistency vs availability (CAP theorem), and rate limiting. The most-recommended resource is 'Designing Data-Intensive Applications' by Martin Kleppmann, often called the DDIA book. For interview prep specifically, the 'System Design Interview' books by Alex Xu (volumes 1 and 2) are excellent and concise.

Common system design patterns to memorize and apply: Use a CDN for static assets and edge caching. Use Redis or Memcached for hot data caching with eviction policies (LRU, LFU). Use a load balancer (nginx, AWS ALB, or Envoy) to distribute traffic. Use horizontal sharding to scale write throughput when a single database can't handle it. Use read replicas to scale read throughput. Use a message queue to decouple producers from consumers and handle async work. Use CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation) when read and write patterns diverge significantly. Use event sourcing when you need an audit log and replay capability. Practice 10-15 common designs: URL shortener, Twitter timeline, ride-sharing dispatch, video streaming, distributed cache, chat application, payment processing, search autocomplete, web crawler, recommendation system.

Free system design learning resources: System Design Primer on GitHub (donnemartin/system-design-primer) is the canonical free resource with 280k+ stars. ByteByteGo's YouTube channel publishes weekly system design videos with great diagrams. High Scalability blog (highscalability.com) has real architecture case studies from companies like Netflix, Uber, and Airbnb. Engineering blogs from these companies are gold—read posts from Netflix Tech Blog, Uber Engineering, Airbnb Engineering, and Discord's blog. For practice, do mock interviews with peers and watch system design interview videos on YouTube where senior engineers solve problems live (channels: 'Hello Interview', 'Jordan has no life', 'codeKarle').
